body:
@xxrlzzz Hi, I'm testing trackpad on win->macOS.

I find trackpad will not work correctly because macOS check if `ControlKey::Scroll` is in the modifiers.

https://github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk/blob/1ededa3476aab868364f6853706e91b7b0ad4e4c/src/server/input_service.rs#LL841C32-L841C32

`check_scroll_on_mac` is only compiled on macOS.

So, I have some questions:

1. Why `check_scroll_on_mac` is needed ? `mask & 7 == 4` is not enough to know whether current event is trackpad event?
2. I cann't understand the logic after `let btn = mask >> 3;`  in `check_scroll_on_mac`. Can you please explain or give a link of what you refered?
   I've revert the code to Commit `260c924`, but I cannot find where `0xff88`, `0x78`, `0x780000` are from.

https://github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk/blob/260c924010361137dd67be145e9d5c0850804768/src/flutter_ffi.rs#L840


`handler.send_mouse` in 
https://github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk/blob/260c924010361137dd67be145e9d5c0850804768/src/ui/remote.tis

body:
Flutter's [latest release](https://docs.flutter.dev/get-started/install/linux) contains flutter@3.7.3 and dart@2.19.2

In [pubspec.yaml](https://github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk/blob/master/flutter/pubspec.yaml) file there's no upperbound for dart, refer to `environment.sdk` value. Therefore while resolving dependencies, flutter automatically created the upperbound in `pubspec.lock` file to `3.0.0` for dart i.e at `2.19.2`.

body:
@rustdesk 

To avoid this kind of problem with not English language  it's better to use specific strings and not use same string for more than one context.

Because in Italian/French/German (etc) a strings can be male/female/neutral.
Then to have a unique string can create errors in not English language

Ex. the English string "Auto" (Automatic) in italian could be "Automatico" (male) or "Automatica" (female) depend by context.

To avoid this issue in language file for this context use the string "Automatic codec" (it's specific).

Thanks.

body:
Highlevel: 
As mentioned by newly added debug messages auth- file will be extraxcted from Xorg process and for a relative path home path will be added.

If no HOME var was found the home folder for given user will be determined from system config (new function: get_user_home_by_name)  old logic just used hard coded  default pattern "/home/<username>" (now only if no system config was found)  which wasn't working for domain users (AD, samba, freeipa, ...) where an additional domain subfolder (/home/<domain>/<username>) needs to be added.

In addition detected file needs to exist, if not this loop won't detect any auth- file. So next step can detect a valid file.

Next step (Todo): Detect auth file by checking process tree (processes below Xorg) without need of predefined checks for Gname, XFCE, Plasma, ...). But as this was my first modifiaction using rust language, there was too less time for better solution.

body: The ability to establish a NATS MQ connection via a user-specified socks5 proxy has not yet been implemented. This is mainly because [nats.rs](https://github.com/nats-io/nats.rs) doesn't expose the necessary API. Instead, it just using `TcpStream::connect` directly ([async-nats/src/connector.rs](https://github.com/nats-io/nats.rs/blob/c661d2147ccbbfc25a32fad8e5b9c1cb9ca077df/async-nats/src/connector.rs#L291C27-L291C27)). We might need to fork our own version to replace it with other `dyn AsyncWrite + AsyncRead + Send + Unpin`, e.g., getting from `FramedStream`.

author: rustdesk

body:
In #6628 I skipped supported resolutions on wayland as it doesn't work on wayland and it was making calls to `get_capturables` on loop and hence user prompts on loop.

But in this PR I have changed `get_capturables` so that it makes only one user prompt request no matter how many calls it gets. https://github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk/commit/eff2490081c48c4f251847de5024cb8d0e2a8c1e
So reverting #6628 enables the future possibility of supporting `supported resolutions` on wayland with only one user prompt. Currently, it is working the same with or without it.

body:
1. When the previous connection ended with the option show_all_displays set to "Y", the new connection only displays one display, and the follow function doesn't work until you switch display manaully. Currently it is a runtime state not a config. the recommended approach is to use a session-level variable state instead of relying on the previous connection's settings. 

2. Opt:  Follow feature is used in the case of having only one display. When `displays_as_individual_windows` is enabled, there may be one display. If there are two displays, 'displays_as_individual_windows' can be deselected, so `displays_as_individual_windows ` can't represent display count. Because `displays_as_individual_windows` is only permitted when texture render is enabled, you can check the number of `map_display_sessions`  in flutter.rs to verify.

body: Instead of using the current_display in the peer info, it would be better to use a separate new message. Currently, the client handles it in `sync_peer_info` message, but the current_display doesn't actually change until the client sends a switch display command. So, it's not a true representation of peer information. What this message does is to send command to request the client to switch display, rather than sync the future display info.

body: No size (resolution)? Can we restore resolution after connect next time? Have you confirmed we have exactly the same behavior as ToDesk?
author: rustdesk

body:
> No size (resolution)? Can we restore resolution after connect next time?

Yes.
The resolutions in system `Display settings` (`HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers\Connectivity`) are used.

ToDesk also use the system `Display settings`.

> Have you confirmed we have exactly the same behavior as ToDesk?

Not exactly.

1. ToDesk does not allow multiple control side, it requires external channel plug-in.
2. We can't control the virtual display index. So there is a drawback.

![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/fc55595d-15d0-4e21-acd0-229085bb417a)
author: fufesou

body:
> The resolutions in system `Display settings` (`HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers\Connectivity`) are used.

You have unplugged (all virtual displays of amyuni idd will be deleted), the resolution info (e.g. the user changes the virtual display resolution manually from our toolbar) still there?
author: rustdesk

body:
Yes.

The display settings are stored in registry `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\GraphicsDrivers\Connectivity`.
The displays settings will be applied for the same display combinations.

Eg. You have two displays `A`, `B`, you have ever set the display settings like:
1. `A`, `1920x1080`
2. `B`, `1440x900`
3. `A+B`, `2560x1440` and `1024x768`

Then, you have plugged out all displays
1. Plug in `A`, the resolution is `1920x1080`
2. Plug in `B`, the resolutions will be `2560x1440` and `1024x768`
3. Plug out `A`, the resolutions is `1440x900`



For `amyuni` idd, the resolutions are just changed without recording

https://github.com/rustdesk/rustdesk/blob/ce56be65077602d6fa4713ecb8d80d7ce6e71d42/src/server/connection.rs#L2711

Then, the controlled side will never try recovery the resolution set by self.

The plugged in display will always apply the system display settings. (The combination of the displays).

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR addresses the remote printer feature by updating the installation option and ensuring correct registry updates on Windows. Key changes include:  
- Adding a #[cfg(target_os = "windows")] check in the server startup to call check_update_printer_option.  
- Introducing new functions (check_update_printer_option and update_install_option) in src/platform/windows.rs to update the printer installation option via the registry.  
- Extending the ipc::Data enum and its handler to support an InstallOption variant, with an associated setter call from the Flutter FFI layer.

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

| File                         | Description                                                                                   |
| ---------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| src/server.rs                | Inserts a Windows-specific call in the server start flow to update the printer install option. |
| src/platform/windows.rs      | Makes REG_NAME_INSTALL_PRINTER public and adds functions for checking and updating this option. |
| src/ipc.rs                   | Adds a new InstallOption variant and extends the message handler to update the registry option. |
| src/flutter_ffi.rs           | Invokes set_install_option based on the success status from a Flutter operation.                |

<details>
<summary>Files not reviewed (1)</summary>

* **flutter/lib/desktop/pages/desktop_setting_page.dart**: Language not supported
</details>

<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (1)</summary>

**src/platform/windows.rs:1617**
* The command string in update_install_option includes extra newlines and leading spaces, which may lead to unintended behavior during execution; consider trimming the string to ensure a cleaner command.
```
        "\nchcp 65001\nreg add HKEY_CLASSES_ROOT\.{ext} /f /v {k} /t REG_SZ /d \"{v}\"\n    "
```
</details>

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR fixes the remote printer feature by updating the install option after enabling the remote printer and extends support for the printer option update on Windows. Key changes include adding a call on Windows in the server startup routine, exposing the printer registry constant as public in the Windows platform module, and adding the new InstallOption IPC variant along with an asynchronous function to update the install option.

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

| File                         | Description                                                               |
| ---------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| src/server.rs                | Added a Windows-specific call to update the printer install option during server startup. |
| src/platform/windows.rs      | Made REG_NAME_INSTALL_PRINTER public and added functions to check and update the printer option via the registry. |
| src/ipc.rs                   | Extended the Data enum and handler with a new InstallOption variant.      |
| src/flutter_ffi.rs           | Updated the printer option installation logic via the IPC call.           |

<details>
<summary>Files not reviewed (1)</summary>

* **flutter/lib/desktop/pages/desktop_setting_page.dart**: Language not supported

## Pull Request Overview

This PR improves the handling of logon screen detection on various platforms by introducing platform‐specific checks and functions. Key changes include:
- In connection.rs, replacing direct calls with a new platform‐dependent variable “is_logon” that combines is_prelogin() with is_logon_ui() (Windows) or is_locked() (Linux/MacOS), and handling for Android/iOS.
- In platform/windows.rs, adding a new inline function is_logon_ui() to detect the presence of “LogonUI.exe”.
- In platform/mod.rs, macos.rs, and linux.rs, adding new helper functions (get_pids, is_locked) to support the cross-platform logon detection improvement.

| src/platform/windows.rs       | Introduces is_logon_ui() to determine if “LogonUI.exe” is running on Windows.  |
| src/platform/mod.rs           | Adds get_pids function to support process listing for logon UI detection.     |
| src/platform/macos.rs         | Adds is_locked() to detect lock screen state using ioreg command on macOS.      |

Learn how to get started.

`if format_id.force_relay { ... };` has a trailing semicolon after the `if` expression, which is unnecessary and can trigger clippy’s `needless_semicolon` lint. Consider removing the trailing `;` for cleaner style consistency.

author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
When `server == PUBLIC_SERVER`, this uses `RENDEZVOUS_SERVERS[0].to_string()` without normalizing/ensuring the rendezvous port. `create_online_stream()` requires a `host:port` string, so if `RENDEZVOUS_SERVERS[0]` is hostname-only (as implied by other call sites that wrap it with `check_port(..., RENDEZVOUS_PORT)`), online queries for `@public` peers will fail and mark everything offline. Use the same `check_port(RENDEZVOUS_SERVERS[0], RENDEZVOUS_PORT)` normalization here.
```suggestion
                            check_port(
                                hbb_common::config::RENDEZVOUS_SERVERS[0],
                                hbb_common::config::RENDEZVOUS_PORT,
                            ),
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The per-server `query_online_states_` calls are awaited sequentially. If a user has peers spread across multiple rendezvous servers, total latency becomes the sum of each query timeout (worst-case N * 3s), which can make the UI’s online indicator feel unresponsive. Consider running the per-server queries concurrently (e.g., collect futures and `join_all`/`FuturesUnordered`) and then merge results.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
`create_online_stream()` allows `port == 1`, which will produce `online_server` with port `0` (`port - 1`). It’d be safer to require `port > 1` (or explicitly handle `port <= 1`) before subtracting, to avoid attempting to connect to an invalid port in edge configurations.
```suggestion
            Some((url, port)) if port > 1 => {
```

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_

**This remap table drops duplicate or aliased inputs.**

The inner `HashMap` keeps only one `raw_id` per normalized peer id. If the caller passes multiple entries that normalize to the same `(server, id)` — for example `123456` and `123456/r` — the later insert overwrites the earlier one, so only one card gets updated after the online response is remapped.



Also applies to: 3662-3674

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed.

In `@src/client.rs` around lines 3650 - 3654, The current fold closure that builds
a HashMap of server -> HashMap<id, raw_id> uses .insert(...) which overwrites
duplicates/aliases (e.g., "123456" vs "123456/r"); change the inner map value to
a collection (Vec<String> or HashSet<String>) and push/insert the raw_id instead
of replacing it in the closure that contains
map.entry(server).or_insert(HashMap::new()).insert(id, raw_id). Update the
parallel occurrence around lines 3662-3674 the same way and ensure subsequent
remap logic that consumes this structure iterates over all raw_ids for each
(server,id) rather than assuming a single raw_id.
```

</details>

body:
♻️ Duplicate comments (3)
src/client.rs (3)
`4029-4033`: _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟡 Minor_ **Preserve all raw IDs for a normalized peer.** `insert(id, raw_id)` keeps only the last alias for each normalized peer. If the caller passes both `123456` and `123456/r`, only one card is remapped after the response and the other never updates.
💡 Suggested fix ```diff - .fold(HashMap::new(), |mut map, (raw_id, id, server)| { - map.entry(server) - .or_insert(HashMap::new()) - .insert(id, raw_id); + .fold(HashMap::new(), |mut map, (raw_id, id, server)| { + map.entry(server) + .or_insert_with(HashMap::new) + .entry(id) + .or_insert_with(Vec::new) + .push(raw_id); map }); ``` The remap logic in the spawned task then needs to flatten all raw IDs for each returned normalized ID instead of assuming a single `String`.
🤖 Prompt for AI Agents ``` Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed. In `@src/client.rs` around lines 4029 - 4033, The current fold closure builds a map using map.entry(server).or_insert(HashMap::new()).insert(id, raw_id) which overwrites aliases so only the last raw_id for a normalized id is preserved; change the structure to collect all raw IDs per normalized id (e.g., map server -> HashMap>) in the same fold so you push raw_id instead of insert, and update the remap logic in the spawned task that consumes this structure to flatten/iterate all raw IDs for each returned normalized ID when remapping cards instead of assuming a single String. ```
--- `3928-3960`: _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🔴 Critical_ **Strip `/r` from the server half before storing the rendezvous host.** `format_id("id@server/r")` still produces `server = "server/r"` and leaves `force_relay` false unless the suffix is on the ID half. That breaks both connection setup and online-state lookup for the PR’s target input form.
💡 Suggested fix ```diff fn format_id(id: &str) -> FormatId { - if id.contains("@") { + if let Some((raw_id, server_and_args)) = id.split_once('@') { let mut force_relay = false; - let mut v = id.split("@"); - let raw_id: &str = v.next().unwrap_or_default(); - let mut server_key = v.next().unwrap_or_default().split('?'); - let server = server_key.next().unwrap_or_default(); + let mut server_key = server_and_args.split('?'); + let server = server_key.next().unwrap_or_default(); + let server = if let Some(server) = + server.strip_suffix("/r").or_else(|| server.strip_suffix(r"\r")) + { + force_relay = true; + server + } else { + server + }; let args = server_key.next().unwrap_or_default(); let key = if server == PUBLIC_SERVER { config::RS_PUB_KEY.to_owned() } else { let mut args_map: HashMap = HashMap::new(); @@ let real_id = crate::ui_interface::handle_relay_id(raw_id).to_string(); if real_id != raw_id { force_relay = true; } FormatId { id: format!("{real_id}@{server}"), server: Some((real_id.to_string(), server.to_string(), key.to_string())), force_relay, } } else { ```
🤖 Prompt for AI Agents ``` Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed. In `@src/client.rs` around lines 3928 - 3960, In format_id, the parsed server half may include a trailing "/r" which currently leaves server = "server/r" and doesn't set force_relay; detect and strip a trailing "/r" from server (e.g., if server.ends_with("/r") then set force_relay = true and server = server.trim_end_matches("/r")), ensure the trimmed server value is used when constructing the FormatId.id string and the server tuple stored in FormatId.server (the tuple created in the server: Some((...)) field), and preserve the existing real_id relay detection (crate::ui_interface::handle_relay_id) by OR-ing its result with the server-suffix detection so force_relay becomes true if either half requested relay. ```
--- `4040-4070`: _⚠️ Potential issue_ | _🟠 Major_ **Treat batch query errors as offline for that server.** The `Err` path only logs. `query_online_states_()` still returns `Err` for timeouts / missing `OnlineResponse`, so every peer behind that rendezvous server can keep a stale status indefinitely instead of being marked offline.
💡 Suggested fix ```diff for (server, map) in group.into_iter() { let ids: Vec = map.keys().map(|t| t.to_string()).collect(); + let raw_ids: Vec = map.values().cloned().collect(); let query_timeout = query_timeout.clone(); join.spawn(async move { match query_online_states_(ids, query_timeout, server.as_str()).await { Ok((on, off)) => { let on: Vec = on @@ let off: Vec = off .into_iter() .filter_map(|id| map.get(&id).cloned()) .collect(); Ok((on, off)) } - Err(e) => Err(e), + Err(e) => { + log::debug!("query onlines error: {}", e); + Ok((Vec::new(), raw_ids)) + } } }); } @@ - Ok(Err(e)) => { - log::debug!("query onlines error: {}", e); - } Err(e) => { log::error!("task panicked: {}", e); } ```
🤖 Prompt for AI Agents ``` Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed. In `@src/client.rs` around lines 4040 - 4070, The batch query currently only logs errors from query_online_states_ and returns Err, leaving peers behind that server with stale statuses; update the join.spawn closure so that when query_online_states_(...).await returns Err(e) you return Ok((Vec::new(), map.values().cloned().collect())) (i.e., treat all peers from that server as offline) and optionally log the error, rather than propagating Err(e); this uses the existing closure captures (ids and map) and keeps the outer join.join_next handling the unified Ok(Ok((on, off))) path. ```
🤖 Prompt for all review comments with AI agents ``` Verify each finding against the current code and only fix it if needed. Consider using a `Duration` constant (e.g., `const MSG_DUP_MAX_INTERVAL: Duration = Duration::from_millis(100);`) and comparing `Instant::elapsed() < MSG_DUP_MAX_INTERVAL` to improve clarity and avoid repeated `as_millis` conversions.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR addresses the issue of missed connection audit logs by ensuring that audit POST requests are sent one after another in a dedicated background loop.

- Introduces a new unbounded mpsc channel (`tx_post_seq`) and a `post_seq_loop` task to serialize audit requests.
- Updates `post_conn_audit` to enqueue requests instead of spawning them concurrently.


<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (2)</summary>

**src/server/connection.rs:252**
* [nitpick] The field name `tx_post_seq` is a bit generic. Renaming it to something like `audit_request_tx` or `seq_post_audit_tx` would make its purpose clearer.
```
    tx_post_seq: mpsc::UnboundedSender<(String, Value)>,
```
**src/server/connection.rs:975**
* [nitpick] Public-facing helper methods like `post_seq_loop` would benefit from a doc comment explaining its behavior, shutdown semantics, and what types of messages it processes.
```
    async fn post_seq_loop(mut rx: mpsc::UnboundedReceiver<(String, Value)>) {
```
</details>
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Using an unbounded channel can lead to unbounded memory growth if the receiver falls behind. Consider using a bounded channel (e.g., `mpsc::channel`) or applying backpressure to prevent unbounded queuing under high load.
```suggestion
        let (tx_post_seq, rx_post_seq) = mpsc::channel(100); // Bounded channel with capacity 100
```

## Pull Request Overview

This PR ensures the server won’t restart on Linux if active terminal sessions exist and fixes a join deadlock by sending a final newline and properly terminating child processes after threads exit.

- Adds an `exiting` flag, `stop()` helper, and `Drop` impl in `TerminalSession` to cleanly shut down threads and kill the child process.
- Introduces `get_sessions_count` in the terminal service, IPC support for `TerminalSessionCount`, and a client helper `get_terminal_session_count`.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR ensures terminal sessions block automatic server restarts on Linux and fixes a deadlock when shutting down terminal child processes by sending a final newline, joining reader/writer threads, and then killing the process.

- Adds `get_terminal_sessions_count` on Linux and skips server restart when sessions exist.
- Implements `stop()` with an `AtomicBool`, sends `\r\n`, joins threads, and kills the child in `TerminalSession`.
- Extends IPC with `TerminalSessionCount` and an async `get_terminal_session_count()`.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR prevents the server from restarting when active terminal sessions exist and ensures terminal child processes and I/O threads are cleanly shut down on Windows and Linux.

- Add an `exiting` flag and `stop()` method in `TerminalSession` to send a final newline, join reader/writer threads, and kill the child.
- Introduce `get_terminal_session_count` on Linux and integrate it into the server restart check.
- Extend IPC to support `TerminalSessionCount` requests and implement a client helper to fetch the count synchronously.

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

| File                             | Description                                                   |
| -------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------- |
| src/server/terminal_service.rs   | Added `exiting` flag, `stop()` method, and proper `Drop` impl |
| src/platform/linux.rs            | Early-return in `should_start_server` when sessions > 0       |
| src/ipc.rs                       | Added `Data::TerminalSessionCount` variant and helper method  |


<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (4)</summary>

**src/ipc.rs:286**
* [nitpick] The `get_terminal_session_count` function in `terminal_service` and this IPC variant share the same name but live in different contexts. To avoid confusion between client-side and server-side logic, consider renaming the IPC helper (e.g., `FetchTerminalSessionCount`) or the enum variant.
```
    TerminalSessionCount(usize),
```
**src/ipc.rs:648**
* [nitpick] This variant is used both as a request (client sends) and as a response (server returns), but the incoming payload is ignored. This dual use can be confusing; consider separating request and response enum variants to make the protocol clearer.
```
        Data::TerminalSessionCount(_) => {
```
**src/platform/linux.rs:373**
* Using `unwrap_or(0)` here will silently ignore IPC errors and treat failures as zero sessions. Consider matching on the `Result` to log or handle errors explicitly so you don't mask underlying connectivity issues.
```
        let terminal_session_count = crate::ipc::get_terminal_session_count().unwrap_or(0);
```
**src/ipc.rs:1399**
* Annotating a library function with `#[tokio::main]` will create and tear down a Tokio runtime on each call, which can be costly. Consider using an existing runtime or `Runtime::new().block_on(...)` once rather than spawning a new runtime repeatedly.
```
#[tokio::main(flavor = "current_thread")]
```
</details>

- Introduce `TerminalService` and extend `TerminalServiceProxy` to carry an optional `user_token` and apply it when spawning Windows shells  
- Implement Windows platform helpers (`get_logon_user_token`, `ensure_primary_token`, `is_user_token_admin`) and integrate them into the `Connection` flow for administrator checks  
- Update the Flutter application with new CLI flags, environment-variable handling, dialog updates, and menu entries for “Run as administrator”

## Pull Request Overview

This PR updates the macOS-specific `caffeinate` invocation in `create_tcp_connection()` to retain the child process handle, aiming to prevent defunct (zombie) processes.

- Wraps the `spawn()` call in an `if let` and stores the `Child` in `CHILD_PROCESS`
- Splits the `-t 5` argument into separate `arg("-t")` and `arg("5")` calls
- Retains the wake-up log outside the spawn block
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The log statement is outside the spawn success block, so it will always log 'wake up macos' even if spawning caffeinate fails. Consider moving this inside the `if let` block and adding an `Err` branch to log spawn failures.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Storing the child handle prevents dropping it but does not reap the process, which can still become defunct. Consider spawning a background thread or async task to call `task.wait()` once the process exits.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Spawn errors are currently ignored. Add an `else if let Err(e)` branch to log any spawn failures for easier debugging.

## Pull Request Overview

This PR implements functionality to activate existing terminal windows when connecting to the same remote peer ID, rather than always creating new terminal windows. The change improves user experience by bringing existing connections to the foreground when available.

- Adds logic to check existing terminal windows for active sessions before creating new ones
- Implements session matching based on remote peer ID to identify existing connections
- Prioritizes recently used terminal windows by iterating in reverse order

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| flutter/lib/utils/multi_window_manager.dart | Adds iteration through existing terminal windows to find active sessions for the same remote ID |
| flutter/lib/desktop/pages/terminal_tab_page.dart | Implements handler for session activation requests and window focusing logic |
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The code uses `startsWith` to match remote IDs, which could cause false positives. For example, if you have remote IDs 'abc123' and 'abc1234', connecting to 'abc1234' would incorrectly match the 'abc123' session. Consider using exact string matching or a more precise matching algorithm.
```suggestion
        if (currentTab.key == call.arguments) {
```

body:

```suggestion
            "Expected String arguments for kWindowEventActiveSession, got ${call.arguments.runtimeType}");
```

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR updates the KCP connection handling to address potential crashes and improve connection close detection. The changes focus on ensuring proper close notifications are sent and received, especially when the controlling side closes the connection.

- Move CloseReason handling earlier in the message processing flow to ensure unauthorized connections can receive close notifications
- Add close reason sending for KCP connections with a delay to ensure message delivery
- Track sent close reason state to prevent duplicate messages

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| src/server/connection.rs | Moves CloseReason message handling to occur before authorization checks |
| src/client/io_loop.rs | Adds KCP-specific close reason sending with delay and tracking state |
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

## Pull Request Overview

This PR updates the KCP protocol handling to improve connection stability and ensure proper connection closure detection. The changes address potential crashes in KCP frame handling and improve close reason communication between controlling and controlled endpoints.

- Move CloseReason message handling earlier in the message processing pipeline to ensure it's received even when unauthorized
- Add logic to force send close reasons for KCP connections with a 30ms delay to ensure message delivery
- Track close reason sending status to prevent duplicate messages

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| src/server/connection.rs | Moves CloseReason handling to the beginning of message processing to ensure proper handling for unauthorized KCP connections |
| src/client/io_loop.rs | Adds close reason tracking, KCP-specific close handling with delay, and refactors close reason sending logic |


## Pull Request Overview

This PR fixes printer printable area issues and configures Windows services to use delayed auto-start. The changes ensure services start automatically with a delay after system boot, which can improve system startup performance and reliability.

- Updated printer driver to version 4-1.4 to resolve printable area issues
- Added delayed auto-start configuration for Windows services in both exe and MSI installations
- Modified service creation scripts and C++ custom actions to include delayed start functionality

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| src/platform/windows.rs | Added `sc config` commands to set delayed auto-start for services |
| res/msi/CustomActions/ServiceUtils.cpp | Added Windows API call to configure delayed auto-start during service creation |
| res/msi/CustomActions/CustomActions.cpp | Added shell command execution to configure delayed auto-start as fallback |
| .github/workflows/flutter-build.yml | Updated printer driver download URL and checksums to version 4-1.4 |

## Pull Request Overview

This PR fixes a bug where the timeout value was not being properly shared between the main thread and spawned tasks. The issue was that modifications to a u64 timeout variable inside spawned tasks didn't affect the timeout value used outside those tasks.

- Replaced mutable u64 timeout variable with Arc<RwLock<u64>> for thread-safe sharing
- Added proper cloning and dereferencing to access the timeout value across thread boundaries
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Using unwrap() on a write lock can cause a panic if the lock is poisoned. Consider using expect() with a descriptive error message or proper error handling.
```suggestion
                                *timeout.write().expect("Failed to acquire write lock on timeout due to poisoning") = 3000;
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Using unwrap() on a read lock can cause a panic if the lock is poisoned. Consider using expect() with a descriptive error message or proper error handling.
```suggestion
            let timeout = *timeout.read().expect("Failed to acquire read lock on timeout. The lock may be poisoned.");
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

## Pull Request Overview

This PR addresses macOS-specific warnings and refactors code organization by suppressing compiler warnings on macOS, replacing `whoami::hostname()` with a custom `whoami_hostname()` function, and cleaning up unused code. The refactoring primarily involves reorganizing imports, adding conditional compilation attributes, and introducing better warning suppression.

Key changes:
- Introduces `whoami_hostname()` wrapper function to replace direct `whoami::hostname()` calls
- Adds `#[allow(dead_code)]` and `#[allow(unused_variables)]` attributes to suppress warnings

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR implements functionality to distinguish direct IPv6 connections from relay connections in the connection handling logic. The main purpose is to properly identify when an IPv6 connection should be treated as direct even when it follows the relay process flow.

- Adds a new `Option<bool>` parameter to track direct connection status independent of the relay process
- Updates connection logic to treat IPv6 connections as direct connections while maintaining the relay process flow
- Modifies function signatures and return types to accommodate the new direct connection tracking
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The logic for determining direct connection status is complex and would benefit from a more detailed comment explaining when x.2 would be Some(false) versus None, and how this relates to the IPv6 direct connection handling.
```suggestion
                /*
                 * Direct connection status logic:
                 * - x.0.1: Indicates whether the connection is physically direct (true if no relay is used).
                 * - x.2: Option<bool> representing the direct process status:
                 *     - None: The connection did not go through the relay process, so direct status is determined by x.0.1.
                 *     - Some(false): The connection used the relay process, but in the case of IPv6, this can still be a direct connection
                 *       (since IPv6 direct connections may follow the relay process for negotiation, but are not relayed in practice).
                 *     - Some(true): (Unused/invalid in current logic; if present, would indicate a direct process connection.)
                 * 
                 * In summary:
                 * - For IPv6 connections, RelayResponse may indicate a direct connection that followed the relay process.
                 *   In this case, x.0.1 is true (physically direct), but x.2 is Some(false) (relay process used).
                 * - For all other cases, x.2 is None and x.0.1 determines directness.
                 * - The logic below ensures that direct status is correctly inferred for both IPv4 and IPv6 scenarios.
                 */
```

body: The function arbitrarily selects the first display info using .values().next() rather than using a specific display. This could return inconsistent results depending on HashMap iteration order and doesn't align with the multi-display architecture.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Same issue as get_displays() - this function randomly selects the first available display info instead of using a deterministic method to get the primary display information.

body: The `parse::<i32>()` call is missing error handling. This will cause a panic if the parsing fails, even though the surrounding code expects error handling via `Result`.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: ## Pull Request Overview Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 5 comments. --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: PIPEWIRE_INITIALIZED is set to true before successfully creating all per-display capturers. If any Capturer::new(...) call fails afterwards, this function returns early with CAP_DISPLAY_INFO still empty and PIPEWIRE_INITIALIZED stuck at true, preventing subsequent initialization and leaking any partially created state. Move setting PIPEWIRE_INITIALIZED to true until after all capturers are created and inserted (or guard it with a rollback on error).
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Use drain() to consume entries while freeing to avoid iterating over a map you then clear, and to make it impossible to accidentally reuse the freed pointers. Example: for (_, addr) in write_lock.drain() { /* free */ } // no need to call clear().
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Storing pointers as u64 is error-prone and obscures ownership/lifetime; prefer storing Box<CapDisplayInfo> (or NonNull/Arc) directly in the HashMap to let Rust manage memory and avoid transmute-like casts.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: This writes a raw pointer as u64 into the map, which then requires manual Box::from_raw in clear(). Consider storing Box<CapDisplayInfo> directly (HashMap<usize, Box<CapDisplayInfo>>) so freeing happens automatically and dereferencing is safer.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: rects and displays are identical across all CapDisplayInfo instances but are cloned per display, increasing memory usage linearly with the number of monitors. Consider wrapping these in Arc to share the same data across instances (e.g., Arc<Vec<_>>), avoiding repeated cloning.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
`parse::<i32>()` returns `Result<i32, ParseIntError>`, it does not painic.

```rust
pub enum IntErrorKind {
    Empty,
    InvalidDigit,
    PosOverflow,
    NegOverflow,
    Zero,
}
```
author: fufesou

body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR fixes WebSocket reconnection logic to handle specific connection reset scenarios that were previously preventing automatic reconnection. The fix allows reconnection when a connection is reset without properly closing the handshake, which can occur during user switching scenarios. ### Key Changes - Modified the retry condition logic to allow reconnection for "connection reset without closing handshake" errors when using WebSocket connections - Added specific handling for tungstenite WebSocket library error patterns --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The complex boolean logic with nested conditions is difficult to read and maintain. Consider extracting the handshake error check into a separate function or variable to improve readability.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
## Pull Request Overview This PR adds virtual mouse functionality for mobile clients when connecting to Desktop peers in touch mode. The virtual mouse provides on-screen mouse controls to enable better interaction with desktop environments from mobile devices. Key changes: - Adds a new "Show virtual mouse" option in the remote toolbar for mobile touch mode - Implements a comprehensive floating mouse widget with left/right click, scroll wheel, and auto-canvas scrolling - Integrates new localization entries for the "Show virtual mouse" feature across all supported languages ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 53 out of 53 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | flutter/lib/mobile/widgets/floating_mouse.dart | New comprehensive floating mouse widget implementation with mouse simulation and canvas scrolling | | flutter/lib/models/model.dart | Adds virtual mouse state management and touch blocking functionality | | flutter/lib/models/input_model.dart | Refactors mouse handling to support virtual mouse with canvas movement control | | flutter/lib/mobile/pages/remote_page.dart | Integrates floating mouse widget in mobile remote page | | flutter/lib/common/widgets/toolbar.dart | Adds virtual mouse toggle option to mobile toolbar | | src/lang/*.rs | Adds "Show virtual mouse" translation entries across all language files |
--- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR fixes window positioning on Windows when the taskbar is positioned on the top or left edges of the screen by dynamically querying the work area instead of using hardcoded coordinates. - Replaces hardcoded window position (10, 10) with dynamic positioning based on the system work area - Adds new Win32Desktop utility module to query the available work area using SystemParametersInfoA - Calculates window size to ensure it fits within the available work area boundaries ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | flutter/windows/runner/win32_desktop.h | Header file defining the GetWorkArea function interface | | flutter/windows/runner/win32_desktop.cpp | Implementation of GetWorkArea to query system work area using Windows API | | flutter/windows/runner/main.cpp | Updates window positioning logic to use work area-based coordinates | | flutter/windows/runner/CMakeLists.txt | Adds the new win32_desktop.cpp source file to the build | --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Using SystemParametersInfoA (ANSI version) is unnecessary here since no string parameters are involved. Consider using SystemParametersInfo or SystemParametersInfoW for consistency with modern Windows development practices.
```suggestion
    if (!SystemParametersInfo(SPI_GETWORKAREA, 0, &workAreaRect, 0))
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The size calculation `workarea_size.width - origin.x` is incorrect. Since `origin.x` is `workarea_origin.x + 10`, this becomes `workarea_size.width - (workarea_origin.x + 10)`, which could result in negative values if the work area doesn't start at (0,0). The calculation should be `workarea_size.width - 10` and `workarea_size.height - 10` to account for the 10-pixel offset.
```suggestion
  Win32Window::Size size(min(800u, workarea_size.width - 10), min(600u, workarea_size.height - 10));
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
There is no function `SystemParametersInfo`. It's just a macro that calls either `SystemParametersInfoA` or `SystemParametersInfoW`. The parameter in this instance doesn't involve any string values at all, so it is irrelevant which entrypoint is used. I noticed that other existing code uses the ANSI functions for Win32 API calls, such as:

* `LoadLibraryA` in `main.cpp`, `win32_window.cpp`
* `GetEnvironmentVariableA` in `wf_cliprdr.c`
* `LoadLibraryExA`, `GetModuleHandleExA`, `LoadCursorA`, `RegisterClassExA`, `CreateWindowExA`, `FindWindowExA` in  `mag.rs`
* `FormatMessageA` in `lddController.c`
* `ShellExecuteA` in `virtual_display_manager.rs`

These existing ANSI calls were what I immediately saw in the surrounding code, so I used `SystemParametersInfoA` to follow suit.

But, I do recognize that using the wide-character functions is generally preferable. It avoids an extra layer of indirection in the use of the thunk, and if actual character strings are involved, it also avoids issues where the ANSI code page might not support characters being used, making it impossible to access certain files, properly display messages, etc. *Those concerns are irrelevant to a call to `SystemParametersInfo` with action `SPI_GETWORKAREA`.*

Should I change this call to use the wide-character entrypoint, or leave it using the ANSI endpoint for consistency with adjacent code?
author: logiclrd

body: Good catch. The origin is, at this point, not relative to the work area, but to (0, 0), so if the work area doesn't start at (0, 0), then the math is wrong. This one is insidious, because in the common case, the work area *does* start at (0, 0). But, the bug this code intends to fix is precisely where the work area *doesn't* start at (0, 0).
author: logiclrd

body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR addresses a race condition where window position changes could be lost if the application is closed before the debounced save operation executes. The solution introduces a "dirty" flag tracking system to ensure window frame changes are immediately saved on application close. - Adds a dirty flag (`_frameIsDirty`) to track unsaved window frame changes - Replaces direct debouncer calls with a centralized `_scheduleSaveFrame` method - Ensures immediate frame saving in the `onWindowClose` handler when changes are pending --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.

The null-safety operators are inconsistent. `key.windowId!` uses force unwrapping while `pos.isMaximized ?? false` uses null coalescing. Since `pos` is guaranteed to be non-null at this point, the null coalescing operators for `isMaximized` and `isFullscreen` are unnecessary and may mask potential issues if these fields are unexpectedly null.

```suggestion
          key.type, key.windowId!, pos.isMaximized!, pos.isFullscreen!, pos);
```

body:
The timezone conversion logic is incorrect. The code treats the input as local time but datetime.strptime() creates a naive datetime object. Using timestamp() on a naive datetime assumes local timezone, but this may not produce the expected UTC conversion. Consider using timezone-aware datetime objects or explicitly handling timezone conversion.
```suggestion
        # Make the datetime object timezone-aware using system's local timezone
        local_dt = local_dt.replace(tzinfo=datetime.now().astimezone().tzinfo)
        utc_dt = local_dt.astimezone(timezone.utc)
        return utc_dt.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S.000")
    except ValueError:
        try:
            # Try without microseconds
            local_dt = datetime.strptime(time_str, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S")
            # Make the datetime object timezone-aware using system's local timezone
            local_dt = local_dt.replace(tzinfo=datetime.now().astimezone().tzinfo)
            utc_dt = local_dt.astimezone(timezone.utc)
```

I thought the same but then I asked GPT-5 and it had a convincing argument along these lines:

ViewStyle.scale is synchronous and has no session context. It can’t fetch the per-session custom percent. That’s why it’s a no-op for Custom. Actual scaling is applied in `CanvasModel.updateViewStyle` after reading `kCustomScalePercentKey`, which is why it works at runtime.

body:
I think we should keep the guard (defensive programming).

From my research, it avoids redundant Rx writes + listener notifications and prevents unnecessary UI/state churn.
author: deblasis

body:
I think we should keep this.

Reasons: 
- It’s a local snapshot used only to enable/disable menu items; you update state only on actual toggles, not on every canvas model change.
- It avoids wiring Obx inside submenu items, which can behave poorly while menus are open; a local setState on boolean flip is predictable and light.
- It’s easy to extend (e.g., combine with other gating logic without touching the observable).
- It decouples FutureBuilder/menu composition from GetX reactivity while still reacting to changes via a narrow listener that ignores no-ops (see the next != _scrollEnabled check above).

I fidgeted a lot with these things and learnt about Obx. I was having freezes in the remote connection window before settling to this. I mentioned this in the video as well I think.
author: deblasis

body:
> a single client can select up to 2^64 unique addresses within one connection

Is a threshold of 60 too lenient?
Sorry, I'm not sure, just a question.
author: fufesou

body:
Yeah it's tricky. We have a lot of head-room since an attacker needs 2 billion-ish tries (2^36 * 6) to brute force 6-digit OTP space. So 60 could just as well be 1000. 

But I at least wanted some number >30, the limit for a single IP ban. I'm thinking of unknown unknowns. Maybe this is being used where a /64 is actually a LAN (perhaps some dinky W-ISP) where you wouldn't want others on the LAN to be able to cause you to get locked out.

body:
## Pull Request Overview This PR introduces a new "Edge scrolling" mode that automatically scrolls the view when the mouse cursor approaches the viewport edges, combining the stability of scrollbar mode with the convenience of auto-scrolling. Key changes include implementing platform-specific mouse cursor warping functionality, refactoring ScrollStyle enum handling, and adding edge detection logic. **Key changes:** - Added platform-specific `bumpMouse` functionality via Platform Channel for Windows, Linux, and macOS to warp the mouse cursor position - Refactored `ScrollStyle` enum to include string conversion methods and added the new `scrolledge` value - Implemented edge detection and auto-scroll logic in `CanvasModel` with debounced resize suppression ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 17 out of 17 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

body:
That's not quite the case. The last commit checks `isFinite`, not `isInfinite`. These are not the strict logical inverse of each other. NaN is not infinite, but it is not finite either. `isFinite` returns `false` for both `Infinity` and `NaN`.

```
Value             isFinite  isInfinite  isNaN
----------------  --------  ----------  -----
(numeric)         true      false       false
infinity          false     true        false
negativeInfinity  false     true        false
nan               false     false       true
```
author: logiclrd

body: It's arbitrary and could absolutely be changed. That said, the mouse isn't captured, so the only thing keeping it inside the window is Bump Mouse. If the mouse cursor manages to cross over the edge scrolling region in its entirety in a single event, then it escapes the window and that's that. Set to 100, it takes a concerted effort to make this happen. If it were set to e.g. 10, edge scrolling would be almost useless because casual use of the mouse would be constantly escaping the window. So a balance must be struck.

body:
## Pull Request Overview This PR implements TLS fallback functionality from `native-tls` to `rustls-tls` with caching to improve HTTPS connectivity reliability across platforms, particularly addressing TLS 1.3 handshake failures on Windows, macOS, and iOS. **Key Changes:** - Introduces TLS type caching mechanism via `URL_TLS_TYPE` HashMap to remember which TLS backend works for each domain - Adds `allow-tls-fallback` configuration option to enable automatic fallback behavior - Refactors HTTP client creation to accept URL parameters for TLS type detection and fallback logic ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 11 out of 12 changed files in this pull request and generated 5 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | src/hbbs_http/http_client.rs | Core TLS fallback implementation with new client creation functions that test connections and cache working TLS types | | src/hbbs_http/account.rs | Lazy initialization of HTTP client to support URL-based TLS detection | | src/hbbs_http/record_upload.rs | Updates thread spawning to initialize client with URL-based TLS detection | | src/common.rs | Adds TLS fallback logic to update checking and generic HTTP request functions | | src/updater.rs | Updates to use URL-aware HTTP client creation | | src/hbbs_http/downloader.rs | Updates to use async URL-aware HTTP client creation | | src/hbbs_http.rs | Re-exports new HTTP client creation functions | | flutter/lib/utils/http_service.dart | Changes logic to use Rust HTTP implementation on all non-web platforms for consistency | | Cargo.toml | Consolidates reqwest dependency with both native-tls and rustls-tls features enabled for all platforms | | .github/workflows/flutter-build.yml | Adds libssl-dev dependency for Linux builds | | libs/hbb_common | Updates submodule reference to version with TLS support functions |
--- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.

body: The function acquires a write lock immediately, even when the client might already exist. Consider using a read lock first to check if the client exists, and only upgrade to a write lock if initialization is needed. This pattern (check-lock-check) reduces lock contention.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The body is cloned unnecessarily when TLS type is not cached. This clone happens on every uncached request, potentially copying large payloads. Consider restructuring to avoid cloning by consuming body in the fallback path or using a reference-counted type.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
Same issue as in `post_request`: the body is cloned when TLS type is not cached. This affects all HTTP methods and can be expensive for large request bodies.
```suggestion
        match get_http_response_async(&url, tls_url, &method, body.as_ref().map(|b| b.as_str().to_owned()), &header, tls_type)
            .await?
        {
            Some(resp) => resp,
            None => {
                // Only clone the body if a retry is needed
                let body_for_retry = body.clone();
                get_http_response_async(
                    &url,
                    tls_url,
                    &method,
                    body_for_retry,
                    &header,
                    Some(TlsType::Rustls),
                )
                .await?
                .ok_or_else(|| anyhow!("HTTP request failed!"))?
            }
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The fallback logic performs HEAD requests to test connectivity, but these requests are not using the same TLS URL (`tls_url`) that was used for cache lookup. The HEAD request goes to `url` while the cache lookup and logging use `tls_url`. This could cause cache misses when `url` differs from `tls_url` (e.g., when using a proxy). Use `tls_url` instead of `url` in the HEAD requests for consistency.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: Same issue as Comment 1: the async version also performs HEAD requests to `url` instead of `tls_url`, causing potential cache inconsistency. The HEAD request should use `tls_url` to match the cache key.

body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR fixes a `LateInitializationError` that occurs when attempting to save window position during window closure. The error was caused by uninitialized `position` and `sz` variables in the `saveWindowPosition()` function when window state couldn't be retrieved. Key changes: - Changed `position` and `sz` from `late` non-nullable to nullable types - Added try-catch blocks around `saveWindowPosition()` calls to prevent crashes - Updated validation logic to handle null values appropriately ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | flutter/lib/common.dart | Changed `position` and `sz` to nullable types and updated all dependent logic to handle null cases safely | | flutter/lib/utils/multi_window_manager.dart | Added try-catch block around `saveWindowPosition()` call in window close handler | | flutter/lib/desktop/widgets/tabbar_widget.dart | Added try-catch block around `saveWindowPosition()` calls and reformatted function call | --- **Tip:** Customize your code reviews with copilot-instructions.md. Create the file or learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The repeated use of `position!` within the nested if block is unnecessary since the outer condition already checks `position != null`. Consider using a non-null local variable to avoid repeated null assertions and improve readability.
```suggestion
    final pos = position;
    if (pos.dx < kMinOffset ||
        pos.dy < kMinOffset ||
        pos.dx > kMaxOffset ||
        pos.dy > kMaxOffset) {
```

body:
## Pull Request Overview This PR integrates `rust-platform-verifier` into the Android build to enable proper WSS (WebSocket Secure) certificate verification using the platform's native trust store instead of bundled certificates. Key changes: - Initializes `rustls-platform-verifier` on Android application startup via a new `RustDeskApplication` class - Adds JNI bindings to pass the Android application context to Rust for certificate verification - Configures Gradle build to locate and include the `rustls-platform-verifier-android` Maven artifacts from the Cargo registry ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 9 out of 10 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

body:
This is intented.


>  local control is only regained if `kMouseControlDistance` is exceeded in a single mouse event.

As you guessed,
the user on the controlling side has to move the mouse a significant amount (in a short time) to take control.
author: fufesou

body:
Mm, okay. To me, intuitively, it seemed to make more sense that control would be regained when the mouse had moved more than a certain amount from the starting position. But it's intentional that the mouse position is irrelevant, and that control is regained when the _mouse speed_ exceeds a certain threshold?

If so, then I think two things:

1. The current setting requires me to move the mouse _far_ too quickly for it to be discoverable. I would absolutely just assume that I was locked out and had no way to regain control.

2. The _instantaneous_ mouse speed is probably not the best metric for this. You say that it must move a significant amount "in a short time", but using the instantaneous speed means that the definition of "a short time" is the hardware's reporting interval. I did a quick look-up and haven't verified the numbers, but from what I read, the typical baseline for this is 8 ms, and high-precision gaming mice can have intervals well into the sub-millisecond range.

There's absolutely no way I'm going to move my mouse cursor `kMouseControlDistance` pixels in 125 microseconds.

If the design here (subject to tuning the travel radius) isn't desired, then I propose an alternative: Maintain a queue of timestamped mouse movements as well as the sum of their distance. On each new event that is subject to `selfGetControl` checking, append a new entry and delete any entries outside of a determined interval (say 250 ms). Then, `selfGetControl` is true if the sum exceeds a threshold.

This algorithm will behave identically no matter what direction the mouse is moving (even if the path is curved) and no matter what the rate of mouse position updates is.

body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR adds client-side handling for the 'clear' and 'cls' terminal commands to properly clear the terminal buffer. When users type these commands, the terminal screen is now cleared locally in addition to being sent to the remote terminal. **Key changes:** - Added command buffering logic to detect when 'clear' or 'cls' commands are entered - Clear the terminal buffer locally when these commands are detected --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. Learn how to get started.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The command detection logic only clears the terminal buffer but doesn't prevent the original data from being sent to the remote terminal. This means the 'clear' or 'cls' command is sent to the remote terminal AND the local buffer is cleared, potentially causing inconsistent behavior. The input should still be sent to the remote terminal for proper command execution on the remote side, but the logic should be verified to ensure this is the intended behavior. Additionally, the buffer is cleared on ANY newline, not just when a command is detected, which means multi-character inputs sent in chunks may not be properly accumulated.
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The `_commandBuffer` is cleared immediately when a newline is detected, but this happens before the data variable is potentially modified on line 53 (`data = '\r';`). If the modified data is then added to `_inputBuffer` on line 68, there's a disconnect between what was checked for the clear/cls command and what gets buffered. This could lead to buffered commands not being detected properly when the terminal opens.

body:
Manually clearing the buffer before writing text that contains clear sequences may cause the clear sequences to be processed twice - once by the manual clear and again by `terminal.write()` which should handle ANSI escape sequences natively. This could lead to unintended behavior. Additionally, calling `terminal.buffer.clear()` removes the text unconditionally even if the clear sequence appears in the middle of the output. Consider either letting the xterm library handle the sequences natively, or if manual handling is required, strip the clear sequences from the text before writing: `terminal.write(text)` should be sufficient on its own if the xterm library is functioning correctly.
```suggestion
        // Let xterm handle ANSI escape sequences natively.
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

```suggestion
        if (text.contains('\x1b[2J') || // Clear entire screen, if env TERM = xterm-256color, for consistent behavior
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: The escape sequence `\\x1b[J` (CSI J with no parameter or parameter 0) clears from cursor to end of screen, not the entire screen. Calling `terminal.buffer.clear()` which clears the entire buffer is incorrect for this sequence and will clear content above the cursor that should be preserved. Either handle this sequence separately with appropriate partial clearing, or remove it from this check.

body:
I think I figured out what the deal is with the `macos`-conditional block of directives that were there already and I just overlooked them. I encountered a problem myself with `cargo::rustc-check-cfg` directives, but what I discovered is that the directive was recently migrated to being referred to with two colons `cargo::`, and that it was already there before, just as `cargo:rustc-check-cfg`. I switched the one I added to the older syntax (which is still supported on newer Rust versions), and it works on all the build agents. So, it's not that `rustc-check-cfg` itself requires Rust 1.80+, it's that _using `cargo::rustc-check-cfg` with two colons_ requires `Rust 1.80+`.

Anyway, having both the one-liner and the previous block is completely redundant and, given that it's way more verbose and does unnecessary checks, I removed the older block in favour of the new one-liner.

It also handles a deprecated base64 API warning and fixes a mutability issue with an event loop variable. - Suppresses dead code warnings for FFI-accessible functions and types - Handles deprecated `base64::encode` API with allow attribute - Corrects event loop mutability for non-macOS builds ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 13 out of 13 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

body: ## Pull Request Overview This PR refactors the initialization flow by moving `global_init()` calls and removing crash/panic handler setup from the `global_init()` function. The key change simplifies `global_init()` to only handle Linux Wayland initialization, while moving the initialization call to more appropriate entry points. - Moved `global_init()` call from `main()` to `core_main()` for desktop platforms - Added `global_init()` call to Flutter FFI initialization for mobile platforms - Removed extensive crash/panic handler setup code from `global_init()` function ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments. | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | src/main.rs | Removed `global_init()` call from desktop main function (now handled in `core_main()`) | | src/flutter_ffi.rs | Added `global_init()` call for Android and iOS platforms in the `initialize()` function | | src/core_main.rs | Added `global_init()` call at the beginning of `core_main()` function | | src/common.rs | Removed panic hook and native crash handler setup code from `global_init()` | --- 💡 Add Copilot custom instructions for smarter, more guided reviews. body:
## Pull Request Overview This PR fixes cursor misalignment issues on Wayland systems, particularly for multi-display setups. The fix ensures proper cursor positioning by using logical sizes instead of physical sizes for display and cursor movement, applying scale factors correctly when the controlled side is Linux (Wayland), and addressing scale accuracy issues in the display information exchange between client and server. Key changes: - Modified cursor positioning logic to use logical dimensions and apply scale factors appropriately for Wayland - Changed scale factor transmission from integer to floating-point by sending `scaled_width` for accurate logical scale calculation - Added workaround for GStreamer pipeline crashes when multiple pipelines start simultaneously ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 66 out of 67 changed files in this pull request and generated 4 comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | src/ui_session_interface.rs | Added `scale` parameter to `set_display` interface method | | src/ui/remote.tis | Implemented dual-scale tracking (`display_scale` vs `cursor_scale`) for accurate cursor positioning on Linux/Wayland | | src/ui/remote.rs | Updated Sciter handler to pass scale parameter to `setDisplay` | | src/ui/common.tis | Added delayed message box display for Wayland KDE Plasma tip | | src/server/wayland.rs | Removed unused xrandr-based max resolution calculation, simplified uinput initialization using new `get_desktop_rect` API | | src/server/video_service.rs | Added scale field to display changed message | | src/server/display_service.rs | Enabled scale factor retrieval for Linux non-X11 systems | | src/server/connection.rs | Added display count tracking and Wayland KDE Plasma all-displays warning logic | | src/platform/linux.rs | Reorganized imports (formatting only) | | src/lang/*.rs | Added translation entry for "wayland-kde-plasma-all-displays-tip" message | | src/flutter.rs | Changed scale transmission from integer percentage to logical width for accuracy, added stub parameter to `set_display` | | src/client/io_loop.rs | Updated `set_display` call to include scale parameter | | libs/scrap/src/wayland/pipewire.rs | Added display position filling, logical size fixing, desktop rect calculation, and GStreamer pipeline state change workaround | | libs/scrap/src/common/wayland.rs | Added physical/logical dimension accessors and scale calculation for Wayland displays | | libs/scrap/src/common/linux.rs | Added scale and logical dimension methods to Display enum | | libs/hbb_common | Updated submodule reference | | flutter/lib/models/model.dart | Changed scale calculation from server-sent percentage to computed from `scaled_width`, added display scale usage for Linux peers | | flutter/lib/mobile/pages/remote_page.dart | Applied peer-specific scale factor in image painting | | flutter/lib/desktop/pages/remote_page.dart | Applied per-display scale factors for Linux peers in both non-texture and texture rendering |

```suggestion
            max_x = display.x + size.0 as i32;
```

body:
The function spawns a new thread on each call without joining or tracking it, which could lead to resource leaks if called frequently. According to the comment on line 1838-1839, this is acknowledged as a special case solution. However, consider adding error handling for the unwrap() call, as a poisoned mutex will cause a panic. Use `lock().expect()` with a descriptive message or handle the error explicitly.
```suggestion
        ENIGO.lock().expect("Failed to lock ENIGO mutex in simple_mouse_move_to (mutex may be poisoned)").mouse_move_to(x, y);
```

The feature integrates with audit logging to record session notes both for active disconnections (user-initiated) and passive disconnections (errors without retry). Key changes: - Added a new configuration option "allow-ask-for-note-at-end-of-connection" to enable/disable the feature - Implemented note prompt dialogs that appear when closing connections or on passive disconnection errors - Added automatic audit GUID querying to support note updates for passive disconnections - Removed access token check from `get_audit_server` method ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 70 out of 70 changed files in this pull request and generated 1 comment.

body:
The calculation of `_cellHeight` is incorrect. The parameters passed to the callback are `w` (width in columns), `h` (height in rows), `pw` (pixel width), and `ph` (pixel height). To calculate the height of a single cell/row in pixels, you should divide the total pixel height by the number of rows: `_cellHeight = ph / h`. Currently, the code sets `_cellHeight` to the total pixel height, which will cause incorrect padding calculations in `_calculatePadding()`.
```suggestion
      _cellHeight = h > 0 ? ph / h : null;
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body: In fact, ph is the height of a single line.

body:
Exception here.

Maybe you can wrap `setState` in `WidgetsBinding.instance.addPostFrameCallback()`.

```
[ERROR:flutter/runtime/dart_vm_initializer.cc(41)] Unhandled Exception: Build scheduled during frame.
While the widget tree was being built, laid out, and painted, a new frame was scheduled to rebuild the widget tree.   
This might be because setState() was called from a layout or paint callback. If a change is needed to the widget tree, it should be applied as the tree is being built. Scheduling a change for the subsequent frame instead results in an interface that lags behind by one frame. If this was done to make your build dependent on a size measured at layout time, consider using a LayoutBuilder, CustomSingleChildLayout, or CustomMultiChildLayout. If, on the other hand, the one frame delay is the desired effect, for example because this is an animation, consider scheduling the frame in a post-frame callback using SchedulerBinding.addPostFrameCallback or using an AnimationController to trigger the animation.
```

body:
The shell script variables `app_name`, `app_dir`, and `user` are interpolated directly into the command without proper shell escaping. If these values contain special characters (e.g., spaces, quotes, or shell metacharacters), the command could fail or behave unexpectedly. Consider using shell escaping for these variables, especially `app_dir` which comes from file paths and `user` which comes from system usernames.

For example, you could use the `shell-escape` crate or manually escape these values before interpolation.

body:
The `update_from_dmg` function does not clean up the extracted files in `UPDATE_TEMP_DIR` after calling `update_extracted`. If the update fails or the extracted application is no longer needed, these files will remain on disk. Consider adding cleanup logic after the update process completes or fails.

Additionally, there's no validation that the DMG path exists or is accessible before attempting to extract it.

They call it the other way around: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/visualstudio/ide/reference/command-prompt-powershell?view=visualstudio

Also, markdown formatting.
```suggestion
        - Windows: The build processes underlying Rust crate compilation will require that the Visual C++ build tools be available in the ambient environment. Run these commands from a Visual Studio Developer Command Prompt, or explicitly run `vsvars`.
```
author: workingjubilee

body:
This feels like a... confused phrasing? It is spiritually correct but "`g++` installs" is wrong because the *package manager* is what "installs" anything, and even referring to `g++` as a *package* is... unlikely. The actually-relevant package is probably `gcc`... *or* something like `make-devel` or `build-essential`.

Also, "newest" is somewhat confounding. For "rolling release" distros, you are always at the "newest", every time you update. Another way to phrase this might be...

```suggestion
        > [Linux] NB: If your distro provides gcc 15 for `c++`, then `g++` will not be compatible with all the C++ code used in RustDesk's build and will cause build errors. When an earlier version is installed, however, the `/usr/bin/c++` link might not be configured to run it. You may need to explicitly configure your system's alternatives mechanism, with a command such as `update-alternatives --install /usr/bin/c++ c++ ``which g++-13`` 13`, or manually create a symbolic link from `/usr/bin/c++` to the correct path for a compatible `g++` version.
```

The last also seems like a *bad* suggestion, though. Can't we just "shadow" the distro's `c++` for this build by providing an alternative one earlier in `$PATH`?

So setting

```shell
PATH=/somewhere/my/c++/compiler/for/this/build/lives:${PATH}
```
author: workingjubilee

body:
> The last also seems like a *bad* suggestion, though.

Or, at least, poorly contained. You can set PATH for a single build but it's unlikely someone wants to mess with all their builds ever that reach for a `c++` compiler.
author: workingjubilee

body: This is what I'm going on here: (It used to be named "Developer Command Prompt for Visual Studio"; the name changed with the upgrade to 2026 "Insiders". Trendy name. Plep. 😛)
author: logiclrd

body: As far as I can tell, though, there is no `g++` that means use GCC 13's `g++`. The file that means that is named `g++-13`. It might be possible to locally alias `g++` to `g++-13`, but this isn't inherited, so it'd have to be done in the user's profile. I did a bunch of searches, and from what I was able to find, updating the alternative is, in fact, the canonical way to resolve the issue.
author: logiclrd

body: Hmm, one thought occurs to me -- it isn't an uncommon practice for build scripts to respect environment variables `$CC`, `$CXX`, `$GCC`, `$GXX`. I wonder if it'd work to point them at `gcc-13` / `g++-13`.

body: It does! End-to-end build with `/usr/bin/c++` pointing at Clang but `CXX` pointing at the result of `which g++-13`. Nice.

## Pull Request Overview This PR fixes pagination logic across multiple Python API client scripts. The original implementation incorrectly treated the `current` parameter as an offset (incrementing by `pageSize`) when the API expects it as a 1-indexed page number. Key changes: - Initialize `current` to 0 and increment at the start of the loop to ensure the first API request uses `current=1` - Change the termination condition from offset-based (`current > total`) to page-based (`current * pageSize >= total`) - Remove the incorrect `current += pageSize` increment ### Reviewed Changes Copilot reviewed 5 out of 5 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.
Show a summary per file | File | Description | | ---- | ----------- | | res/users.py | Fixed pagination in `view()` function to use page numbers instead of offsets | | res/user-groups.py | Fixed pagination in `list_groups()` and `view_users()` functions | | res/devices.py | Fixed pagination in `view()` function | | res/device-groups.py | Fixed pagination in `list_groups()` and `view_devices()` functions | | res/ab.py | Fixed pagination in `view_shared_abs()`, `view_ab_peers()`, and `view_ab_rules()` functions |

body:
The comment states "+/- 30% jitter" but the implementation only adds positive jitter (0-30%). The calculation `_random.nextDouble() * 0.3` produces values from 0 to 0.3, which when used in `(1 + jitter)` results in a multiplier of 1.0-1.3 (0% to +30%).

For true "+/-" jitter, the calculation should be:
```dart
final jitter = (_random.nextDouble() - 0.5) * 0.6; // +/- 30% jitter
```
This would produce values from -0.3 to +0.3, giving a multiplier of 0.7-1.3 (-30% to +30%).
```suggestion
      final jitter = (_random.nextDouble() - 0.5) * 0.6; // +/- 30% jitter
```

**Make the LLVM-path selection deterministic.**

The current code at line 316 uses `find /usr/lib/ -name libclang.so | head -n1` which depends on filesystem enumeration order. On builders with multiple LLVM installs, this can select the wrong version instead of the pinned `LLVM_VERSION` defined in the workflow at `.github/workflows/flutter-build.yml` line 27.

Since `yq` is already widely used throughout `flutter/build_fdroid.sh`, the proposed approach of reading the pinned LLVM version from the workflow file and using it to construct a deterministic path lookup (e.g., `find /usr/lib -path "*/llvm-${LLVM_MAJOR}/lib/libclang.so"`) would align the bridge generator's dependency resolution with the workflow's explicit versioning and eliminate the non-deterministic behavior.

<details>
<summary>🔧 More deterministic lookup</summary>

```diff
+	LLVM_VERSION="$(yq -r \
+		.env.LLVM_VERSION \
+		.github/workflows/flutter-build.yml)"
+	LLVM_MAJOR="${LLVM_VERSION%%.*}"
-	BRIDGE_LLVM_PATH="$(find /usr/lib/ -name libclang.so | head -n1)"
+	BRIDGE_LLVM_PATH="$(
+		find /usr/lib -path "*/llvm-${LLVM_MAJOR}/lib/libclang.so" -print -quit 2>/dev/null
+	)"
 
 	if [ -z "${BRIDGE_LLVM_PATH}" ]; then
-		echo 'ERROR: Can not find libclang.so for bridge generator!' >&2
+		echo "ERROR: Can not find libclang.so for LLVM ${LLVM_MAJOR}!" >&2
 		exit 1
 	fi
```
</details>
